metadata.career_relevance: Senior DSA round: 'Find subarray with product closest to k that has no repeating elements.' Requires sliding window (no repeats) + product tracking. These combined problems separate the candidates who memorized patterns from those who understand them.

hook.real_world_problem:
```
Flipkart DSA round (Senior SDE):
'Given trade amounts, find all subarrays where
 no trade repeats AND sum equals target'

Needs TWO patterns:
  Sliding window (no-repeat constraint)
  Prefix sum / two pointers (sum equals target)

Recognizing this combination → gets the offer.
```
hook.what_you_will_build: Solutions to 6 synthesis problems combining multiple array patterns.

concept_explanation.pattern_choice:
**Pattern recognition guide:**

```
Pair in sorted array → Two Pointers (inward)
Contiguous subarray → Sliding Window (fixed or variable)
Range sum queries → Prefix Sums
3 categories sort → Dutch National Flag
Max contiguous sum → Kadane's Algorithm
No extra space → In-place (swaps, two pointers)

Combinations:
  No repeats + some constraint → Sliding window (set) + constraint
  k-sum problems → Sort + (k-2) loops + Two Pointers
  Count subarrays → Prefix sum + HashMap
  Subarray with mixed signs → Prefix sum (not sliding window)
```

visual_aid.description: Decision tree for array patterns. Root: 'What type of problem?' Branch 1: 'Pair/triplet?' → Two Pointers. Branch 2: 'Contiguous subarray?' → Sliding Window. Branch 3: 'Range query?' → Prefix Sum. Branch 4: '3 categories?' → DNF. Branch 5: 'Max sum?' → Kadane. Each branch shows time/space complexity.

step_by_step_solution.details: For each element: either extend the previous subarray (currentSum + num) or start fresh (num), whichever is larger. maxSum = max seen so far.

things_to_notice: currentSum = max(num, currentSum + num): restart when previous sum was negative
O(n) time, O(1) space — simple but powerful
Handles all negatives: maxSum will be the least negative element

industry_examples.use_case: Kadane's on daily P&L array → find best trading period (max gain). Two pointers on sorted trade sizes → find pairs with target profit.

applications.interview_relevance: Recognizing which pattern to apply is 50% of the battle. Practice: for any array problem, ask (1) contiguous? (2) pairs? (3) range queries? (4) 3 categories? Then choose the right tool.
applications.real_world_workflow: Multi-pattern approach: (1) identify all constraints in the problem, (2) map each constraint to a pattern, (3) combine — usually one loop handles multiple patterns simultaneously.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Using sliding window for 'subarray sum == k' when array has negatives. Sliding window requires monotonic property (adding right always increases sum if positives only).
common_gaps.remediation: Negative elements break the sliding window monotonic property. Use prefix sum + HashMap instead. Test: if array can have negatives AND problem involves sum — always use prefix sums, not sliding window.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Student uses sliding window on [-1,1,2,-1] sum==2 and gets wrong answer

key_takeaways: Pattern recognition: pairs→TP, contiguous→SW, range→PS, 3-cat→DNF, max-sum→Kadane
Negatives break sliding window: use prefix sum + HashMap
Product except self: prefix products from left, suffix from right

self_check_questions.question: When does sliding window fail? What should you use instead?
self_check_questions.answer: Sliding window fails when the array contains negative numbers and you're looking for a sum-based condition (like 'subarray sum == k'). The reason: with negatives, adding an element to the window doesn't always increase the sum, and removing from the left doesn't always decrease it. The monotonic property breaks. Solution: use prefix sum + HashMap. The approach: prefix[j] - prefix[i] = k → count how many i's have prefix[i] = prefix[j] - k. This works with any values including negatives.
